title: Whole Numbers and Operations
unitGoal: Students will strengthen their understanding of whole numbers, place value, estimation, and the four operations. They will use strategies to solve problems accurately and explain their thinking clearly.

lessons.title: Place Value
lessons.teacherSummary: Students build place value understanding from ones and tens through hundred thousands.

studentActivity.title: Place Value Quest
studentActivity.mission: Earn practice stars by finding the value of digits.

steps.title: Quick Check
steps.hint: Count ten single ones. They can be bundled into 1 ten.

steps.title: Find the Place
steps.prompt: In 26, the 2 is in which place?
steps.feedback: Correct. The 2 is in the tens place.
steps.hint: In 26, the 6 is in the ones place. The 2 is one place to the left.

steps.title: Find the Tens
steps.prompt: In the number 46, which digit belongs in the tens place?
steps.feedback: Yes. In 46, the 4 is in the tens place.
steps.hint: Look at the digit just to the left of the 6.

steps.title: Try It
steps.feedback: Yes. The 3 tells how many tens.
steps.hint: The 4 is in the ones place. The 3 is in the tens place.

steps.title: Find the Number
steps.hint: Look for the 7 in the tens place.

steps.title: Find the Digit
steps.prompt: Which digit is in the tens place in 58?
steps.feedback: Yes. In 58, the 5 is in the tens place.
steps.hint: The tens place is the digit just to the left of the ones place.

steps.title: Find the Tens
steps.prompt: In the number 72, which digit belongs in the tens place?
steps.hint: In 72, the 7 is in the tens place.

steps.title: True or False
steps.feedback: Correct. The 8 is in the tens place.

steps.title: Level 1 Check
steps.prompt: Which number has 9 tens and 5 ones?

steps.title: Quick Check
steps.hint: A hundred is made from ten groups of ten.

steps.title: Find the Place
steps.prompt: In 142, the 1 is in which place?
steps.feedback: Correct. The 1 is in the hundreds place.
steps.hint: Read the places from right to left: ones, tens, hundreds.

steps.title: Find the Digit
steps.prompt: In 325, which digit tells how many hundreds?
steps.feedback: Yes. The 3 tells how many hundreds.
steps.hint: The hundreds place is the first digit in a three-digit number.

steps.title: Find the Place
steps.feedback: Right. The 3 tells how many hundreds.
steps.hint: In 305, the 3 is in the hundreds place even though there are 0 tens.

steps.title: Find the Tens
steps.prompt: In the number 648, which digit belongs in the tens place?
steps.feedback: Yes. 648 has 6 hundreds, 4 tens, and 8 ones.
steps.hint: The middle digit tells the tens.

steps.title: Expanded Form
steps.feedback: Correct. 276 has 2 hundreds, 7 tens, and 6 ones.
steps.hint: The 2 is worth 200 and the 7 is worth 70.

steps.title: Find the Number
steps.hint: Look for a three-digit number with 4 in the hundreds place.

steps.title: Find the Place
steps.prompt: In 987, the 8 is in which place?
steps.feedback: Correct. The 8 is in the tens place.
steps.hint: The 8 is the middle digit, so it is worth 80.

steps.title: True or False
steps.feedback: Correct. The 2 is in the tens place, so it is worth 20.
steps.hint: The 6 is worth 600, but the 2 is worth 20.

steps.title: Level 2 Check
steps.prompt: Which number has 8 hundreds, 0 tens, and 4 ones?
steps.feedback: Great. 804 has 8 hundreds, 0 tens, and 4 ones.
steps.hint: 0 tens means there is a 0 in the middle.

steps.title: Quick Check
steps.hint: A thousand is ten groups of one hundred.

steps.title: Find the Place
steps.prompt: In 1,426, the 1 is in which place?
steps.feedback: Correct. The 1 is in the thousands place.
steps.hint: From right to left: ones, tens, hundreds, thousands.

steps.title: Find the Digit
steps.prompt: In 3,582, which digit tells how many thousands?
steps.feedback: Yes. The 3 tells how many thousands.
steps.hint: The thousands digit is just left of the hundreds digit.

steps.title: Find the Value
steps.feedback: Right. The 4 is in the thousands place, so it is worth 4,000.

steps.title: Find the Number
steps.hint: Look for 6 in the thousands place, not the ten-thousands place.

steps.title: Find the Place
steps.prompt: In 8,305, the 3 is in which place?
steps.feedback: Yes. The 3 is in the hundreds place.
steps.hint: The 0 is in the tens place, and the 5 is in the ones place.

steps.title: Expanded Form
steps.feedback: Yes. 2,647 is 2 thousands, 6 hundreds, 4 tens, and 7 ones.
steps.hint: Each digit keeps the value of its place.

steps.title: True or False
steps.feedback: Correct. The 7 is in the thousands place, so it is worth 7,000.
steps.hint: 7 hundreds would be 700, but this 7 is in the thousands place.

steps.title: Find the Value
steps.feedback: Right. The 1 is in the hundreds place.

steps.title: Level 3 Check
steps.prompt: Which number has 5 thousands, 0 hundreds, 8 tens, and 6 ones?
steps.feedback: Great. 5,086 has 5 thousands, 0 hundreds, 8 tens, and 6 ones.
steps.hint: A 0 is needed in the hundreds place.

steps.title: Quick Check
steps.hint: When you have ten groups of 1,000, you have 10,000.

steps.title: Find the Place
steps.prompt: In 24,819, the 2 is in which place?
steps.feedback: Correct. The 2 is in the ten-thousands place.
steps.hint: In a five-digit number, the first digit is ten thousands.

steps.title: Find the Digit
steps.prompt: In 63,507, which digit tells how many ten thousands?
steps.feedback: Yes. The 6 tells how many ten thousands.

steps.feedback: Correct. The 4 is in the ten-thousands place.

steps.title: Find the Value
steps.feedback: Right. The 5 is in the thousands place.
steps.hint: The 7 is ten thousands. The 5 is thousands.

steps.title: Level 4 Check
steps.prompt: Which number has 8 ten thousands, 3 thousands, 0 hundreds, 2 tens, and 1 one?
steps.feedback: Great. 83,021 has each digit in the right place.
steps.hint: The number needs to start with 83 and have 0 in the hundreds place.

steps.title: Quick Check
steps.feedback: Yes. 10 ten thousands make 1 hundred thousand.

steps.title: Find the Place
steps.prompt: In 426,819, the 4 is in which place?
steps.feedback: Correct. The 4 is in the hundred-thousands place.
steps.hint: A six-digit number starts with the hundred-thousands place.

steps.title: Find the Digit
steps.prompt: In 305,728, which digit tells how many hundred thousands?
steps.feedback: Yes. The 3 tells how many hundred thousands.
steps.hint: The first digit in 305,728 is in the hundred-thousands place.

steps.title: Find the Value
steps.feedback: Right. The 7 is in the hundred-thousands place.

steps.title: Find the Number
steps.hint: Look for a six-digit number that starts with 2.

steps.title: Find the Place
steps.prompt: In 518,904, the 1 is in which place?
steps.feedback: Yes. The 1 is in the ten-thousands place.
steps.hint: The 5 is hundred thousands, and the 1 is ten thousands.

steps.title: Expanded Form
steps.feedback: Yes. 684,032 has 6 hundred thousands, 8 ten thousands, 4 thousands, 0 hundreds, 3 tens, and 2 ones.
steps.hint: The 6 is worth 600,000 and the 8 is worth 80,000.

steps.title: True or False
steps.feedback: Correct. The 2 is in the ten-thousands place, so it is worth 20,000.
steps.hint: The 2 is one place left of thousands, so it is ten thousands.

steps.title: Find the Value
steps.feedback: Right. The 7 is in the ten-thousands place.
steps.hint: The 4 is hundred thousands. The 7 is ten thousands.

steps.title: Level 5 Check
steps.prompt: Which number has 5 hundred thousands, 0 ten thousands, 6 thousands, 4 hundreds, 0 tens, and 9 ones?
steps.feedback: Excellent. 506,409 keeps every digit in the right place.
steps.hint: The number starts with 50, then has 6 thousands, 4 hundreds, 0 tens, and 9 ones.

successCriteria: I can name the place of a digit from ones to hundred thousands.
I can tell what a digit is worth.
I can build and read numbers using place value.
lessons.teacherOverview: Short student game focused on place value from ones through hundred thousands, using quick checks, digit-place questions, values, and expanded form.

questions.section: Part A: Ones, Tens, and Hundreds

questions.prompt: In 4,819, which digit is in the thousands place?
questions.reviewNote: The thousands place is the first digit in a four-digit number.

questions.prompt: In 58,216, which digit is in the ten thousands place?
questions.reviewNote: The ten thousands place is the first digit in a five-digit number.
A digit in the ten thousands place is worth that many ten thousands.

questions.prompt: Which number has 6 ten thousands, 1 thousand, 4 hundreds, 0 tens, and 8 ones?
questions.reviewNote: Read the places from left to right: ten thousands, thousands, hundreds, tens, ones.
The 3 is worth 30,000 and the 2 is worth 2,000.
The 5 is in the thousands place, so it is worth 5,000.

questions.prompt: In 482,615, which digit is in the hundred thousands place?
questions.reviewNote: The hundred thousands place is the first digit in a six-digit number.
A digit in the hundred thousands place is worth that many hundred thousands.

questions.reviewNote: A 0 in the ten thousands place still holds that place.
The zeros mean there are no ten thousands and no ones.
The 1 is in the ten thousands place, so it is worth 10,000.

lessons.title: Reading and Writing Large Numbers
lessons.learningGoal: Students will read and write large whole numbers in standard form, word form, and expanded form.
successCriteria: I can read large numbers by looking at each period.
I can write numbers from word form into standard form.
I can write numbers in expanded form.
I can use zeros as placeholders when a place has no value.
lessons.teacherOverview: Student mission focused on reading and writing numbers to the hundred thousands using standard form, word form, expanded form, commas, and zeros as placeholders.

lessons.title: Comparing and Ordering Numbers
lessons.learningGoal: Students will compare and order whole numbers using place value, number length, and careful digit-by-digit checking.
successCriteria: I can compare numbers using greater than, less than, and equal to.
I can use place value to decide which number is larger or smaller.
I can order numbers from least to greatest and greatest to least.
I can explain why one number is greater or less than another.
lessons.teacherOverview: Student mission focused on comparing and ordering whole numbers to the hundred thousands using place value, number length, symbols, least/greatest language, and ordered lists.

quiz.title: Comparing and Ordering Numbers Final Quiz
quiz.instructions: Answer each question one time. Your score will be shown after you submit the quiz.

questions.reviewNote: Compare the last three digits because 68 thousand is the same.
307,015 is less than 307,150 because 015 is less than 150.
94,806 is less because 806 is less than 860.
Equal numbers have the exact same digits in each place.
780 is greater than 708.
Compare the ten-thousands digit: 5 is greater than 1.
90,045 is the smallest because it has fewer thousands than 99,045 and fewer digits than 900,045.

questions.prompt: Which number has the greatest value?
questions.reviewNote: 280,070 has 8 ten-thousands, more than the others.

lessons.title: Rounding and Estimating
lessons.learningGoal: Students will round whole numbers and use rounded numbers to make reasonable estimates.
successCriteria: I can round numbers to the nearest ten, hundred, and thousand.
I can look at the digit to the right of the place I am rounding to.

questions.reviewNote: There are 12 full boxes and 5 pencils left over. 13 full boxes would need 130 pencils.

studentActivity.title: Problem Solver Mission
studentActivity.mission: Earn practice stars by choosing a plan, solving in steps, and checking that each answer makes sense.

steps.title: Choose the First Step
steps.prompt: Mia has 36 stickers. She buys 18 more, then gives 20 away. What should she do first?
steps.feedback: Correct. First find how many stickers she has after buying more.

steps.title: Choose the Operations
steps.prompt: A class has 4 boxes of 12 pencils and 9 extra pencils. Which operations are needed?
steps.feedback: Correct. Find 4 groups of 12, then add 9.
steps.hint: Boxes of the same size usually means multiplication.

steps.title: Question Clue
steps.prompt: A bus has 48 seats. There are 35 students and 6 adults. What is the problem asking?
steps.feedback: Correct. It asks what remains after people sit down.
steps.hint: Look for what the final question asks.

steps.title: Hidden Step
steps.prompt: A store had 90 apples. It sold 24 in the morning and 31 in the afternoon. What hidden step helps?
steps.feedback: Correct. First find how many apples were sold altogether.

steps.title: Pick the Equation
steps.prompt: A teacher makes 6 groups of 8 cards, then adds 5 more cards. Which equation matches?
steps.feedback: Correct. Make 6 equal groups, then add 5.
steps.hint: Use parentheses for the first step.

steps.title: Estimate the Answer
steps.prompt: Luca buys items for $48 and $31. About how much did he spend?
steps.feedback: Correct. 48 is close to 50 and 31 is close to 30.

steps.title: Reasonable or Not
steps.prompt: A school has 9 classes with about 28 students each. Is 2,500 students reasonable?
steps.feedback: Correct. 10 groups of 30 is about 300, not 2,500.

steps.title: Level Check
steps.prompt: Nora has 120 beads. She uses 35 red beads and 28 blue beads. What should she find first?
steps.feedback: Correct. First find the total beads used.
steps.hint: The two used amounts go together.

steps.title: Two-Step Difference
steps.prompt: A library had 245 books on a shelf. Students borrowed 68, then returned 19. How many books are on the shelf now?
steps.hint: Subtract borrowed books, then add returned books.

steps.title: Combine Then Subtract
steps.prompt: A goal is 500 cans. One class collects 138 cans and another collects 207. How many more cans are needed?

steps.title: Compare Totals
steps.prompt: Team A scores 126 points and then 98 points. Team B scores 240 points. How many more points does Team B have?

steps.title: Money Story
steps.prompt: A student saves $75, gets $40 for a birthday, then spends $28. How much money is left?
steps.hint: Money received is added, money spent is subtracted.

steps.title: More Than One Step
steps.prompt: A garden has 340 seeds. 125 are planted Monday and 96 Tuesday. How many seeds are left?

steps.title: Missing Amount
steps.prompt: A fundraiser needs $600. It has $245 and then earns $189 more. How much more is needed?
steps.hint: Find how much they have now.

steps.title: Check the Order
steps.prompt: A tank has 850 L of water. 275 L are used, then 190 L are added. How much water is in the tank?
steps.hint: The story says use first, then add.

steps.title: Level Check
steps.prompt: A store starts with 1,000 flyers. It gives out 325 in the morning and 418 in the afternoon. How many are left?

steps.title: Equal Groups First
steps.prompt: There are 7 boxes with 24 markers in each box. The teacher gives away 35 markers. How many are left?

steps.title: Share Then Add
steps.prompt: 96 cards are shared equally among 8 players. Each player also gets 3 bonus cards. How many cards does each player get?

steps.title: Groups and Extras
steps.prompt: A bakery packs 18 muffins in each tray. There are 9 trays and 14 extra muffins. How many muffins are there?
steps.hint: Multiply trays first, then add extras.

steps.title: Divide Then Compare
steps.prompt: 180 stickers are shared equally among 6 students. Sam has 42 stickers. How many fewer stickers does one student get than Sam?

steps.title: Multiply Then Subtract
steps.prompt: A theatre has 12 rows with 25 seats in each row. If 64 seats are empty, how many seats are filled?

steps.title: Use Division
steps.prompt: A teacher has 144 sheets of paper. She puts 12 sheets in each folder and keeps 4 folders. How many sheets are in those folders?
steps.feedback: Correct. 4 folders with 12 sheets each is 48.
steps.hint: The 144 is extra information for this question.

steps.title: Remainder Context
steps.prompt: 50 students ride in vans that hold 8 students each. How many vans are needed?
steps.feedback: Correct. 6 vans hold 48 students, so 1 more van is needed.
steps.hint: A leftover student still needs a seat.

steps.title: Level Check
steps.prompt: A farm has 15 baskets with 18 apples each. It sells 95 apples. How many apples are left?

steps.title: Three Steps
steps.prompt: A school orders 8 boxes of 30 notebooks and 6 boxes of 25 notebooks. How many notebooks are ordered?
steps.hint: Find each group total, then add.

steps.title: Choose the Better Answer
steps.prompt: A trip costs $18 per student for 24 students. The class has $500. About how much money will be left?
steps.feedback: Correct. 18 × 24 is about 20 × 25 = 500, but exact cost is 432, leaving 68.
steps.hint: Estimate first, then think about the exact amount.

steps.title: Two Groups
steps.prompt: A coach buys 5 packs of 16 cones and 3 packs of 12 cones. How many cones does the coach buy?

steps.title: Leftover Meaning
steps.prompt: 123 books are packed into boxes of 10. How many full boxes and books left over?
steps.feedback: Correct. 12 full boxes hold 120 books, with 3 left.

steps.title: Compare Plans
steps.prompt: Plan A costs $145 plus $38. Plan B costs $200. How much cheaper is Plan B or Plan A?
steps.feedback: Correct. Plan A costs $183, which is $17 less than $200.

steps.title: Estimate Check
steps.prompt: A store sells 29 packs with 21 cards in each pack. Is 609 a reasonable total?

steps.title: Find the Missing Step
steps.prompt: A class needs 240 points. They earn 9 badges worth 18 points each. What should they do after 9 × 18?
steps.feedback: Correct. After finding points earned, subtract from the goal.
steps.hint: The question asks how many more points are needed.

steps.title: Mission Check
steps.prompt: A club has 420 tickets. It sells 6 groups of 45 tickets. How many tickets are left?
steps.hint: Multiply the groups sold first, then subtract.

lessons.title: Review
lessons.teacherOverview: This review activity mixes the major ideas from Grade 5 Math Unit 1. It is intended as practice before the Unit Test and should help students switch between strategies instead of practising one skill at a time.
practiceIdeas: Assign the review mission before the Unit Test.
Use the final review quiz to see which lesson topics need one more practice round.
Encourage students to estimate before calculating when the numbers are large.

quiz.title: Unit 1 Review Quiz
quiz.instructions: Answer each review question carefully. This quiz mixes skills from the whole unit. You will see your score and review notes at the end.

questions.prompt: In 735,842, what is the value of the 3?
questions.reviewNote: The 3 is in the ten thousands place.
Compare from left to right.
The hundreds digit is 4, so the thousands stay the same.

questions.prompt: 504,060 has a 4 in the thousands place.
questions.reviewNote: The 4 is in the thousands place.
Use each place-value part.
Line up place values and add.
Regroup carefully across zeros.
49 is one less than 50, so subtract one group of 6.
Round both numbers to friendly numbers.
Choose parts that divide easily by 6.
47 ÷ 5 = 9 R2. A remainder must be less than the divisor.
Use multiplication to check division.
89 × 7 is close to 90 × 7 = 630.

questions.prompt: A school buys 9 boxes of 36 notebooks and gives away 85. How many notebooks are left?
A fundraiser goal is $1,200. Students raise $475 and then $386. How much more is needed?
If 43 students need vans that hold 8 students each, 5 vans are enough.
questions.reviewNote: 5 vans hold 40 students, so 6 vans are needed.

questions.prompt: A store has 12 shelves with 28 books each and 19 extra books. How many books are there?
When a word problem has equal groups, multiplication or division may be needed.
questions.reviewNote: Equal groups often point to multiplication or division.

lessons.learningGoal: Students will review place value, rounding, estimation, the four operations, and multi-step word problems from Unit 1.
successCriteria: I can read, compare, round, and write whole numbers.
I can use addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division strategies.
I can estimate and check whether my answer is reasonable.
I can solve multi-step word problems by choosing the correct operations.
lessons.lessonContent: Students complete a mixed review mission covering number sense, addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, estimation, and multi-step word problems.

studentActivity.title: Unit 1 Review Mission
studentActivity.mission: Earn practice stars by reviewing whole numbers, operations, estimates, and word problems from Unit 1.

steps.title: Place Value
steps.prompt: In 482,615, what is the value of the 8?
steps.feedback: Correct. The 8 is in the ten thousands place.
steps.hint: Read the place from right to left.

steps.title: Standard Form
steps.hint: Line up each part by place value.

steps.title: Order Numbers
steps.prompt: Which list is in order from least to greatest?
steps.feedback: Correct. The numbers grow from smallest to largest.
steps.hint: Compare thousands first, then hundreds, tens, and ones.

steps.title: Estimate
steps.prompt: Which is the best estimate for 398 + 612?
steps.feedback: Correct. 398 is close to 400 and 612 is close to 600.

steps.title: Expanded Form
steps.hint: Only include place values that are present.

steps.title: Level Check
steps.prompt: Which number has a 6 in the thousands place?
steps.feedback: Correct. In 146,205, the 6 is in the thousands place.
steps.hint: Find the thousands place before choosing.

steps.title: Friendly Addition
steps.feedback: Correct. Move 3 from 86 to make 397 into 400.

steps.title: Choose the Operation
steps.prompt: A school had 1,250 tickets and sold 875. Which operation finds how many are left?
steps.hint: Think about whether the amount goes down.

steps.title: Estimate Difference
steps.feedback: Correct. The exact answer should be near 7,000.

steps.title: Compensation
steps.prompt: Which is a good way to solve 702 - 398?
steps.feedback: Correct. Adding 2 to both numbers keeps the difference the same.

steps.title: Level Check
steps.prompt: A goal is 2,000 points. A team has 1,375. How many more points are needed?

steps.title: Equal Groups
steps.prompt: There are 9 boxes with 18 books in each box. How many books are there?

steps.title: Remainder
steps.hint: The remainder must be less than the divisor.

steps.title: Estimate Product
steps.hint: Use a nearby multiple of ten.

steps.title: Plan First
steps.prompt: A club buys 7 packs of 24 pencils and gives away 53 pencils. How many pencils are left?

steps.title: Share and Add
steps.prompt: 144 cards are shared among 12 players. Each player gets 5 extra cards. How many cards does each player get?

steps.title: Compare After Adding
steps.prompt: Team A scores 235 and 189 points. Team B scores 450. How many more points does Team B have?
steps.feedback: Correct. Team A has 424 points, and 450 - 424 = 26.

steps.title: Use Remainder Meaning
steps.prompt: 38 students need tables. Each table seats 6 students. How many tables are needed?
steps.feedback: Correct. 6 tables seat 36 students, so 1 more table is needed.
steps.hint: A leftover student still needs a seat.

steps.title: Estimate to Check
steps.prompt: A theatre has 21 rows with 29 seats each. Is 609 a reasonable total?

steps.title: Find What Is Needed
steps.prompt: A class needs 1,000 points. They earn 18 badges worth 35 points each. How many more points are needed?

steps.title: Multi-Step Money
steps.prompt: A student has $250. She buys 3 items for $48 each. How much money is left?

steps.title: Review Mission Check
steps.prompt: A store has 900 flyers. It gives 8 groups of 75 flyers away. How many flyers are left?

lessons.title: Unit Test
lessons.teacherOverview: This 30-question Unit 1 test is designed as the formal assessment for Whole Numbers and Operations. The recommended unit mark weighting is 40% lesson/review quizzes and 60% unit test.

assessment.title: Unit 1 Test: Whole Numbers and Operations

questions.prompt: In 684,209, what is the value of the 8?
questions.reviewNote: The 8 is in the ten thousands place.

questions.prompt: Which number is six hundred five thousand, seventy-two?
questions.reviewNote: Six hundred five thousand, seventy-two is 605,072.
Compare digits from left to right.

questions.prompt: Which list is ordered from least to greatest?
questions.reviewNote: Least to greatest means the numbers increase.

questions.prompt: Round 386,451 to the nearest ten thousand.
questions.reviewNote: The thousands digit is 6, so 386,451 rounds to 390,000.
Use the value of each digit.

questions.prompt: Which estimate best checks 48,905 + 31,286 using nearest ten-thousands?
questions.reviewNote: To estimate to the nearest ten-thousands, 48,905 rounds to 50,000 and 31,286 rounds to 30,000.

questions.prompt: In 920,415, the 2 is in the ten thousands place.
questions.reviewNote: The 2 is in the ten thousands place.
Line up place values and add.
Move 2 from 247 to make 698 into 700.
Regroup carefully across zeros.

questions.prompt: Which expression keeps the same difference as 702 - 398?
questions.reviewNote: Add 2 to both numbers to make 398 into 400.

questions.prompt: The answer to 9,812 - 3,106 should be close to 7,000.

questions.reviewNote: 49 is one less than 50.
Break 14 into 10 and 4.

questions.prompt: Which split uses two friendly parts to solve 288 ÷ 8?
questions.reviewNote: 240 and 48 both divide easily by 8, and together they make 288.
8 × 8 = 64 with 3 left over.

questions.prompt: A remainder should always be larger than the divisor.
questions.reviewNote: A remainder must be less than the divisor.

questions.prompt: Multiplication can be used to check a division answer.
questions.reviewNote: For example, 96 ÷ 6 = 16 can be checked with 16 × 6 = 96.

questions.prompt: A school buys 8 boxes of 32 markers and gives away 79 markers. How many markers are left?
A fundraiser goal is $1,500. Students raise $685 and then $429. How much more money is needed?
156 cards are shared equally among 12 players. Each player gets 4 extra cards. How many cards does each player get?
46 students need vans. Each van holds 8 students. How many vans are needed?
questions.reviewNote: 5 vans hold only 40 students, so 6 vans are needed.

questions.prompt: A store has 15 shelves with 28 books on each shelf and 37 extra books. How many books are there?
A trip costs $19 per student for 31 students. Which estimate best checks the total cost?
questions.reviewNote: 19 × 31 is close to 20 × 30.

questions.prompt: In a multi-step problem, the first number you see is always the first number you should use.
questions.reviewNote: You need to read the whole problem and decide what is being asked.

questions.prompt: If your exact answer is much larger than your estimate, your estimate must be wrong.
questions.reviewNote: A big difference means you should check both your estimate and your exact work. The exact answer may be the mistake.

lessons.learningGoal: Students will show their understanding of whole numbers, place value, rounding, estimation, the four operations, and multi-step word problems from Unit 1.
successCriteria: I can use place value to read, write, compare, order, and round whole numbers.
I can add, subtract, multiply, and divide whole numbers using strategies.
I can estimate and check whether my answers are reasonable.
I can solve multi-step word problems and interpret remainders in context.
